forecasts.reasoning: John Morgan has publicly stated he prefers a short campaign, explicitly noting on May 14, 2025, that he'd "rather have a three month window than an 18-month window" for running for governor. He also said he is "waiting to see how the gubernatorial field shapes up." Both of these statements strongly contradict the idea that he would officially announce his candidacy before July 1, 2025 (which would be 16 months before the November 2026 general election). While there is a June 12, 2025 deadline to formally establish and register as a member of a new political party if he wants to run under that party's banner, this only requires him to legally register the party and his voter registration. It does not legally require him to file for or announce his actual candidacy for governor. Given his history of long public flirtations with running (such as in 2017) that didn't culminate in campaigns, and his very clear intention to delay a decision, an announcement before July 1, 2025 is highly unlikely.

The Bulgargaz-Botas agreement has been highly controversial since it was signed, with Bulgargaz paying roughly $500,000 per day for unused capacity and verging on bankruptcy by 2024–2025. However, there is strong evidence that the contract will not be cancelled by July 1, 2025. As of mid-to-late May 2025, statements from the Bulgarian Energy Minister and MPs explicitly emphasized that the contract contains no termination clause and cannot be terminated unilaterally without paying a penalty of over 4 billion levs (approx $2.3 billion or 3 billion levs depending on the source)—a sum Bulgaria cannot afford. Furthermore, the Bulgarian Council of Ministers guaranteed the deal, so even if Bulgargaz declares bankruptcy, the state is still liable, meaning bankruptcy wouldn't clear the contract. Botas has previously rejected attempts at renegotiation and is threatening arbitration. Given these structural legal impediments and the fact that there are only 25 days remaining between the knowledge cutoff (June 6, 2025) and the resolution date (July 1, 2025), a sudden official announcement of cancellation is highly improbable.

Sudan is still in the middle of a devastating civil war that began in April 2023. While the AfCFTA agreement has been ratified by 48 African countries as of early 2025, Sudan remains one of the few that have yet to complete the ratification process. As recently as February 2024, Sudan had not submitted its tariff offers, and with the governance system massively disrupted, there is no legislative or administrative capacity currently focused on ratifying regional trade agreements. With only a month left between the June 2025 cutoff and the July 2025 resolution date, the probability of sudden ratification is extremely low. Therefore, the likelihood of this occurring before July 1, 2025, rests at the minimum bound.
